title: podman remote fails when .ssh/known_hosts contains outdated ciphers
body:
### Issue Description

**podman info** fails when **.ssh/known_hosts** contains unknown ciphers


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. add an unknown cipher to **.ssh/known_hosts** (e.g. for macOS 15  192.168.10.10 SHA256:sv4XrPwL60H)

2. run **podman info**

Expected: Unknown Ciphers skipped
Real: Error above





### Describe the results you received

```
>podman info           
OS: darwin/arm64
buildOrigin: brew
provider: applehv
version: 5.4.0

Cannot connect to Podman. Please verify your connection to the Linux system using `podman system connection list`, or try `podman machine init` and `podman machine start` to manage a new Linux VM
Error: unable to connect to Podman socket: knownhosts: /Users/xx/.ssh/known_hosts:4: knownhosts: missing key type pattern

```


### Describe the results you expected

```
>podman info           
podman info
Client:
  APIVersion: 5.4.0
  BuildOrigin: brew
  Built: 1739290083
  BuiltTime: Tue Feb 11 17:08:03 2025
[...]
```

comments.body: It looks like you installed podman from Homebrew. Can you try installing from the pkginstaller and see if that works? Installing from Homebrew is not a supported method. Thanks!
if you repeat it with the installer, can you share the offending line?

uninstalled via homebrew and installed with the installer => same issue

Putting this in line 1 of .ssh/known_hosts

`
192.168.1.1 SHA256:7b248364ac549a605dc9ee9ef75a3581ec95b58817119d11d01bb2acc7992c8e
`

results in 
```
podman info
OS: darwin/arm64
buildOrigin: pkginstaller
provider: applehv
version: 5.4.0

Cannot connect to Podman. Please verify your connection to the Linux system using `podman system connection list`, or try `podman machine init` and `podman machine start` to manage a new Linux VM
Error: unable to connect to Podman socket: knownhosts: /Users/xx/.ssh/known_hosts:1: knownhosts: missing key type pattern
```

I also get this error now. 
Fun thing though - it worked earlier today on the same version 5.3.2 from brew.

Now it does not work on either 5.3.2 or 5.4.0 from pkginstaller

**Edit**: it works when I removed known_hosts and reconnected and accepted. Did not keep track of before values unfortunately. Key has been the same ed25519 all the time

it adds 3 lines to known hosts:
192.168.1.2 ssh-ed25519 
192.168.1.2 ssh-rsa 
192.168.1.2 ecdsa-sha2-nistp256

and that is working fine

Seeing same issue with
```
$ podman info
OS: darwin/arm64
buildOrigin: pkginstaller
provider: applehv
version: 5.4.0

Cannot connect to Podman. Please verify your connection to the Linux system using `podman system connection list`, or try `podman machine init` and `podman machine start` to manage a new Linux VM

Error: unable to connect to Podman socket: knownhosts: /Users/vanderwlus.ibm.com/.ssh/known_hosts:1: knownhosts: missing key type pattern
```
my knownhosts file is large, I don't see any `192.168.1.x` in there.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

caught this too on MSYS podman

```
$ podman -v
podman.exe version 5.3.2
```
Also running into this issue. Will check which ciphers are offending and report back.

After some trial and error, I've narrowed down the problematic entry in my `known_hosts` file:

```
my.hostname.here ssh-rsa
```

It seems podman expects a hash after the `ssh-rsa` portion. For `ssh`, this isn't explicitly required. Having a line like this without a hash is perfectly valid.

On the `.ssh/known_hosts` problematic line, for me it showed:

` ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 $KEY_STRING` . No preceeding hostname or IP.

I had a lot of entries in `~/.ssh/known_hosts` that occupied two lines, one for the key and one for the host, like:

```
AAAAE2VjZHNhLXNoYTItbmlzdHAyNTYzzzzzzzzzzzSENjQEezOmxkZMy7opKgwFB9nkt5YRrYMjNuG5N87uRgg6CLrbo5wAdT/y6v0mKV0U2w0WZ2YB/++Tpockg= 
10.1.20.45 ssh-ed25519 
```

About 250 of them.

Though for me they are all old, like at least 1.5 years.

So I removed them manually and kept only `ssh-ed25519` and `ssh-rsa` entries that were single line.

And `podman --remote info` was able to execute without the error:
```
Cannot connect to Podman. Please verify your connection to the Linux system using `podman system connection list`, or try `podman machine init` and `podman machine start` to manage a new Linux VM
Error: unable to connect to Podman socket: knownhosts: /Users/[me]/.ssh/known_hosts:1: knownhosts: missing host pattern
```

Thanks for raising this issue, it helped solve why I couldn't use `podman compose` locally without the known_hosts pattern match issue.

For info/posterity/future readers, the problem with my known_hosts was an old line that was connected to the same host as some new lines. So I had:

```
<ip addr.53> SHA256:<hash>
<** loads of lines of others **>
<ip addr.53> ssh-ed25519 <hash>
<ip addr.53> ssh-rsa <hash>
<ip addr.53> ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 <hash>
```

So removing the first SHA256 line fixed it for me. I would have expected it to ignore lines that it wasn't interested in or that didn't fit the pattern, and this wasn't a problem in the past. My guess is that it became a problem when I replaced the device with another one with the same IP address, which added the new lines. However, of course it could have been that I haven't tried to run podman commands for a couple of months and an updated broke it.

My `podman -v` is showing `5.7.1` but I don't know when I updated that or what it was previously. I generally keep it up to date though.

Hope it helps!

For me podman did not stop to add a line without a host to known_hosts and failed.
My problem was, that I configured the remote connection with a hostname and the connection was also in my `.ssh/config`.

It worked, when I configured the remote connection with an IP.

`podman --loglevel=trace ps` gvae me the information to solve this issue.

I've hit the same issue. Thanks all, could have taken a lot longer to solve without this thread. For me it was a line in ~/.ssh/known_hosts with just a key, no idea how/why it got there, but commenting it out worked.

Just thought I'd add a reminder for anyone coming in fresh that `# comments` work in known_hosts, much better than deleting keys while you hunt down the offending line.  Might seem obvious, but hopefully helps someone.
